text: Hello, sharing a robotic tap umbilical hernia repair. It's actually an incisional hernia.
The patient had a previous cholecystectomy and hernia is at the umbilical port site.
Tap has become my go-to for this as I try to keep mesh out of the abdomen.
The surgeon observes the thin peritoneum while carefully initiating the robotic tap umbilical hernia repair, ensuring not to damage the ipsilateral side of the flap.
The surgeon carefully dissects in the midline, gently manipulating tissues with minimal tension to navigate around the visceral preperitoneal fat.
I usually will start probably five centimeters to the ipsilateral side.
The surgeon uses a 15x15 mesh, extending the flap farther to the contralateral side to reduce tension and ensure coverage over the hernia defect at the umbilical port site.
Again, every patient is different.
The surgeon reaches the preperitoneal fat plane, where the tissue dissects bluntly and more easily, revealing the underlying anatomical structures.
The surgeon returns to the area of the hernia near the umbilicus, where the tissue appears more scarred due to the presence of an incisional hernia.

And it can often be hard to keep the peritoneal sac intact when you're reducing incisional hernias. But I always try to make an effort to keep it intact because I don't like to have big holes that need to be closed that might end up putting tension on the flap during closure.
The surgeon performs a slow and careful sharp dissection around the hernia site, aiming to avoid creating holes in the tissue.
The surgeon dissects tissue above and below the hernia, crossing the midline to approach the hernia directly, aiming to expose the 'volcano' area for further surgical intervention.
The surgeon navigates outside the repartineal midline fat on the contralateral side, revealing the very thin peritoneum on the opposite side.
The surgeon carefully dissects along the posterior sheath and transversalis fascia, avoiding grasping the delicate tissue to prevent creating holes during the hernia repair.
The surgeon carefully manipulates the thin peritoneal tissue around the hernia without using grasping instruments, gently pushing down with the left hand to avoid creating holes.
The surgeon prepares to reduce the hernia sac, carefully attempting to remove as much peritoneum from the defect as possible.
trying not to make holes to the best of my ability.

The surgeon continues the dissection on the contralateral side, attempting to recruit as much peritoneum as possible despite the presence of holes, to ensure a more redundant tissue closure of the peritoneal defect.
These cases take me about one hour. Like I said, I will almost always use a fifteen by fifteen mesh. My patients do get tap blocks and nobody or almost nobody goes home with any narcotics.

The surgeon continues dissection on the contralateral side, using blunt technique to separate tissue layers. The peritoneal flap is gently pushed down with the left hand, avoiding grasping to prevent tearing.

The surgeon creates a larger pocket than the fifteen by fifteen mesh size to ensure the peritoneal flap can be brought across the ipsilateral side without tension, effectively covering the mesh outside the peritoneal defect.
The surgeon continues dissection on the contralateral side, aiming to reduce tension and achieve redundancy in the peritoneal flap.
The surgeon sews the posterior peritoneal hole closed, ensuring the closure is secure and covers the defect adequately.
The surgeon attempts to close the posterior peritoneal hole perpendicular to prevent shortening of the flap during closure.
The surgeon prepares to close the small hernia defect, indicating the size of the defect may not significantly impact the closure technique.
The surgeon attempts to imbricate the dermis, folding and suturing the dermal layer to reinforce the closure of the hernia defect.

do decrease the insufflation for these closures in this case i believe i went down to eight for the smaller defects like this i don't know how much it really matters i just don't like the idea of putting these sutures in under tension that theoretically could be loose after desufflation and when the patient starts recruiting and accessing their muscles
The surgeon runs the suture along the length of the incision, using the majority of the suture material provided.
The surgeon places a 15x15 mesh over the hernia defect, securing it with a trio silk suture tied in the middle of the mesh. The suture is positioned in the midline below the defect.
The surgeon places sutures in all four corners of the mesh to secure its edges, preventing clamshelling, bowing, or folding over the hernia defect.
The surgeon proceeds to sew the flap closed, completing the closure of the surgical site.

In this case, I feel like my ports were maybe a centimeter or two too close to the midline, so I was sewing right on the side of myself. You'll see my camera keep dipping inside the port to try to make more room. This is just an error in judgment in terms of port placement. Using the fifteen by fifteen mesh certainly contributes to that.

The surgeon pulls the peritoneal flap to the ipsilateral side, ensuring the mesh remains covered by peritoneum and is not exposed to the abdominal cavity. The mesh is positioned outside the peritoneal pocket as the flap is sewn across it.

There were a couple of sub-centimeter holes that were subsequently closed at the end of the case with a three-oh Vicryl. Patient did well and went home a couple hours after the operation. I hope you enjoyed it.
